title: Do not hide navigation behind hamburger on desktop viewports
summary: AI-generated interfaces default to hamburger-menu navigation on desktop, sacrificing discoverability for visual minimalism.
instruction: Show primary navigation links visibly in the header on viewports wider than 768px. Reserve hamburger menus for narrow viewports where horizontal space is constrained. Navigation should be immediately scannable.
rationale: Hamburger menus reduce navigation discoverability by hiding links behind a click. Users are less likely to explore navigation they cannot see. On desktop viewports where horizontal space exists, visible navigation improves engagement and task completion.
violations: A marketing page at 1440px viewport width with a hamburger menu icon as the only navigation control, hiding Home, Products, Pricing, About, and Contact.
A SaaS dashboard at 1920px with a hamburger menu for primary section navigation (Dashboard, Reports, Settings, Users) when all 4 items would fit in the header.
controls: A marketing page with visible navigation links in the header at desktop width, collapsing to hamburger only below 768px.
A dashboard with a persistent left sidebar showing all section names at desktop, collapsing to icon-only at narrower widths.
borderlineCases: Apps with more than 7 primary navigation items may need to prioritize or use a combination of visible top-level and hamburger for secondary items.
contextualExceptions: Content-forward pages where the entire viewport is dedicated to the reading experience and navigation is genuinely secondary.
Minimalist portfolio or artistic sites where hidden navigation is an intentional aesthetic choice documented in the design direction.
detectorSignal: Hamburger menu icon (three-line SVG or unicode character) used as the primary navigation element on desktop-width viewports
matcherProposal: Detect hamburger icon patterns (three horizontal lines: ☰, SVG with 3 rects, or CSS bars). Check breakpoint where navigation becomes visible. If hamburger is the only navigation at breakpoints >768px AND navigation links exist in the codebase, flag.
detectorLimitations: Hamburger icon can be styled differently across frameworks.
Some navigation systems use JavaScript to toggle visibility and may not appear in static CSS media queries.
Cannot detect user preference for hidden navigation (some users prefer minimal headers).
falsePositiveScenarios: A web app with only 1-2 pages where a hamburger containing just Home and About is legitimate.
An app shell that loads navigation dynamically.
A mobile-first PWA where even desktop viewports use mobile patterns intentionally.
mitigationStrategy: Require at least 3 navigation items to flag. Check breakpoint in responsive CSS. Allow suppression for documented minimal-design directions.

fixturePlans.violation: A 1440px landing page with hamburger icon, 6 nav links hidden behind it, no other navigation visible.
fixturePlans.control: Same landing page with visible nav links at 1440px (Home, Products, Pricing, About, Contact), hamburger appears below 768px.
fixturePlans.borderline: A portfolio site with hamburger at all widths, but only 2 nav items (Work, About) and the design direction documents the minimal approach.

cleanRoomAttestations: Original observation. Usability research on hamburger menus (NN Group).
overlapNotes: Distinct from existing rules. No navigation-specific rule exists in the catalog.
